title: The Autonomic Nervous System: A Plain-English Guide
excerpt: Understand your body's autopilot: the autonomic nervous system's role in trauma, healing, and regulation in clear, accessible language.
body:
What Is the Autonomic Nervous System (ANS)?

The autonomic nervous system (ANS) is the part of your nervous system that runs behind the scenes, controlling all the automatic functions your body does without you thinking about it. Breathing, heart rate, digestion, sweating—all managed by the ANS. It’s your body's autopilot, keeping you alive and adapting you to your environment.

Unlike the voluntary nervous system, which controls muscles you move on purpose, the ANS manages internal organs and glands. This system is critical for survival and plays a huge role in how your body responds to stress and trauma.

Two Main Branches: Sympathetic and Parasympathetic

The ANS has two major branches that act like a gas pedal and a brake:

  • Sympathetic Nervous System (SNS): This activates your body's fight, flight, or freeze response. It gears you up to handle danger by increasing heart rate, pumping blood to muscles, and releasing adrenaline.
  • Parasympathetic Nervous System (PNS): This calms you down, promoting rest, digestion, and repair. It slows your heart rate and encourages relaxation.

These two systems work in tandem, constantly balancing each other out. When you’re relaxed, your parasympathetic system is dominant; when threatened, the sympathetic system takes over.

Stephen Porges and the Polyvagal Theory: A New Lens

Dr. Stephen Porges revolutionized how we understand the ANS with his Polyvagal Theory. Unlike the traditional view of two branches, Porges identified a third pathway within the parasympathetic system: the vagal complex. This adds nuance to how our bodies respond to safety and danger.

The vagus nerve, a major nerve in the PNS, has two pathways:

  • Dorsal Vagal Complex (DVC): Associated with immobilization or shutdown responses—think of freezing or dissociation.
  • Ventral Vagal Complex (VVC): Connected to social engagement, safety, and calming states.

This third pathway explains why sometimes our bodies respond to threat by shutting down rather than activating fight or flight.

Why Understanding the ANS Matters for Trauma and Healing

Trauma isn’t just a psychological event—it’s a physiological one rooted in the ANS. Bessel van der Kolk, in his seminal work The Body Keeps the Score, emphasizes that trauma disrupts the nervous system’s regulation. Traumatized bodies often get stuck in sympathetic hyperarousal (constant fight or flight) or dorsal vagal shutdown (freeze or collapse).

When your ANS is dysregulated, you might feel anxious, numb, hypervigilant, or disconnected—often without knowing why. This is why talk therapy alone may not fully resolve trauma; the body needs to feel safe and learn new patterns of regulation.

Somatic Approaches to Regulating the ANS

Somatic healing focuses on tuning into bodily sensations and helping the nervous system find balance. Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, teaches that trauma responses are held in the body and can be released by gently renegotiating these sensations.

Deb Dana, a leading clinician trained by Porges, offers practical tools for working with the ANS, such as tracking your state and shifting between ventral vagal (safe, social) and sympathetic (alert) states, rather than getting stuck in freeze.

  • Tracking your nervous system: Notice when your heart races, your breath changes, or your muscles tense.
  • Anchoring in safety: Engage your ventral vagal system with deep breathing, soothing sounds, or social connection.
  • Moving gently: Small movements can help discharge sympathetic activation without overwhelming your system.

Internal Family Systems and the ANS

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) model intersects with nervous system work by recognizing that different parts of ourselves hold different emotions and bodily sensations. Some parts may be stuck in fight or flight, others frozen in shutdown. Understanding these parts and their nervous system states can deepen self-compassion and healing.

Practical Tips for Everyday Nervous System Regulation

You don’t need to be an expert to start calming your ANS. Here are some grounded, research-informed strategies:

  • Breathing exercises: Slow, deep, diaphragmatic breathing stimulates the parasympathetic nervous system (Porges, Dana).
  • Safe social connection: Engaging with trusted people activates the ventral vagal pathway, promoting calm (Porges).
  • Movement: Gentle yoga, walking, or stretching can help discharge excess sympathetic activation (Levine).
  • Mindfulness and body awareness: Noticing sensations without judgment fosters nervous system regulation (Siegel, Ogden).
  • Grounding techniques: Using the five senses to connect to the present moment helps interrupt dysregulated states.

When to Seek Professional Help

If your nervous system remains chronically dysregulated despite self-care, working with a trauma-informed somatic therapist can be transformative. Professionals trained in Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy (Pat Ogden), EMDR (Francine Shapiro), or Polyvagal-informed therapy can guide you safely through recalibrating your nervous system.

“Trauma is not what happens to you, but what happens inside you as a result of what happens to you.” – Gabor Maté

This quote reminds us that healing the ANS is key to moving beyond trauma’s grip.

Summary: Your Body’s Hidden Regulator

The autonomic nervous system operates silently, yet it shapes how you feel, react, and heal. By understanding its branches and recognizing your body’s signals, you gain a powerful tool for self-awareness and recovery. The research from Porges, van der Kolk, Levine, Dana, and others provides a science-backed map to reconnect with your body and nervous system. This knowledge shifts healing from just a mental process to a full-body experience.

Remember, your nervous system wants to move toward safety and connection. With patience and somatic practice, you can help it find balance again.

faqs.q: What exactly does the autonomic nervous system control?
faqs.a: The autonomic nervous system controls involuntary bodily functions like heart rate, digestion, respiration, and glandular activity, all without conscious effort.

faqs.q: How does trauma affect the autonomic nervous system?
faqs.a: Trauma can dysregulate the ANS, causing it to get stuck in fight-or-flight or freeze states, leading to symptoms like anxiety, numbness, or hypervigilance.

faqs.q: Can I regulate my autonomic nervous system on my own?
faqs.a: Yes, through somatic practices such as deep breathing, mindful movement, and safe social connection, you can help calm and balance your ANS.

faqs.q: When should I seek professional help for nervous system dysregulation?
faqs.a: If self-care techniques aren’t enough and you experience chronic symptoms like panic, dissociation, or intense emotional swings, working with a trauma-informed somatic therapist can be beneficial.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book explaining how trauma affects the body and how healing involves nervous system regulation.

affiliateProducts.title: Polyvagal Exercises for Safety and Connection by Deb Dana
affiliateProducts.description: Offers practical, science-based exercises to understand and regulate the autonomic nervous system.

affiliateProducts.title: Waking the Tiger by Peter Levine
affiliateProducts.description: Explores somatic experiencing techniques for releasing trauma held in the body.

metaDescription: A clear, research-backed guide to the autonomic nervous system and its role in trauma, healing, and nervous system regulation.
author: The Oracle Lover
